attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command:
python -c "
import zipfile, xml.etree.ElementTree as ET, re

docx_path = r'C:\1stMLProject\hand prediction stroke.docx'

with zipfile.ZipFile(docx_path, 'r') as z:
    with z.open('word/document.xml') as f:
        tree = ET.parse(f)

ns = {'w': 'http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main'}
root = tree.getroot()

paragraphs = []
for para in root.iter('{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}p'):
    texts = []
    for run in para.iter('{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}t'):
        if run.text:
            texts.append(run.text)
    line = ''.join(texts)
    paragraphs.append(line)

full_text = '\n'.join(paragraphs)
print(full_text)
"
input.description: Extract text from .docx using Python zipfile and XML parsing

import sys, zipfile, xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
sys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding='utf-8')

docx_path = r'C:\1stMLProject\hand prediction stroke.docx'

with zipfile.ZipFile(docx_path, 'r') as z:
    with z.open('word/document.xml') as f:
        tree = ET.parse(f)

root = tree.getroot()
paragraphs = []
for para in root.iter('{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}p'):

content.content:
Done

     PART I: PROJECT SUMMARY 

 1. Title of Project :
Development and Validation of a Machine Learning-Based Prediction Model for Hand Function Recovery Using Routine Clinical and CT Data in Patients with Acute Ischemic Stroke


  Summary -\
Upper limb impairment affects 70% stroke survivors, with hand dysfunction being the primary cause of long-term disability. Early accurate prediction of hand recovery is essential for personalized rehabilitation but current tools like PREP2 require TMS/MRI unavailable in most Indian centers. We propose a prospective cohort study to develop a machine learning model predicting 3-month hand function _ARAT≥32_ using only data routinely collected within 72h: NIHSS items, SAFE score, and non-contrast CT. 
350 patients will be recruited for model development and 150 for external validation. XGBoost, Random Forest, and TabNet models will be trained and compared against SAFE score and neurologist judgment. SHAP will ensure interpretability. Primary outcome is AUC-ROC. We hypothesize ML model AUC ≥0.85 vs SAFE ∼0.75. The deliverable is a free web-based calculator for bedside use. This low-cost, scalable tool will enable Indian stroke units to triage patients for intensive hand therapy vs compensatory training by Day 3, optimizing rehab resources and aligning with Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission.

     PART II: TECHNICAL DETAILS 

 ORIGIN OF PROPOSAL 
Stroke is the 2nd leading cause of death and 3rd leading cause of disability in India. Hand recovery determines independence yet is difficult to predict. ICMR’s 2023 Stroke Task Force highlighted need for India-specific prognostic tools. Existing algorithms use TMS/DTI, not feasible in 90% Indian hospitals. ML can leverage routine CT + clinical data for LMIC settings.

 9. RATIONALE OF STUDY 
9.1  National Relevance : 1.8 million new strokes/year in India. Predicting hand outcome early will reduce inappropriate rehab, caregiver burden, and costs.
9.2  Knowledge Gap : No validated hand-specific ML model for Indian population using accessible data. PREP2 not feasible; SAFE score alone has 25% misclassification.
9.3  Hypothesis : Multimodal ML integrating NIHSS, SAFE, and NCCT features will outperform existing bedside tools for predicting ARAT≥32 at 3 months.

 10. OBJECTIVES 

 Primary :
1. To develop and internally validate a machine learning model predicting good hand function _ARAT≥32_ at 3 months post-stroke using baseline clinical and NCCT data

 Secondary :
1. To compare ML model performance with SAFE score, logistic regression, and neurologist prediction
2. To identify key predictive features using SHAP values
3. To externally validate the model in an independent cohort
4. To develop an open-access web calculator for clinical use

 11. METHODOLOGY 

 11.1 Study Design : Prospective observational cohort study with prediction model development & validation. TRIPOD-AI compliant.

 11.2 Study Population :
 Inclusion : Age ≥18 yrs, First-ever acute ischemic stroke, Admission <72h of onset, NIHSS arm motor ≥1, Pre-stroke mRS ≤2, Consent.
 Exclusion : Hemorrhagic stroke, bilateral infarcts, severe aphasia/neglect precluding assessment, terminal illness.

 11.3 Sample Size :
Development: n=350. Expect 40% good outcome = 140 events. Allows 14 predictors at 10 EPV.
External Validation: n=150 from second center.
_Justification_: Riley et al BMJ 2020 criteria for ML prediction models + 20% loss to follow-up.

 11.4 Study Variables :
  Time Point   |   Variables   |   Method  
  Baseline <72h   | Age, Sex, Handedness, NIHSS total + items, SAFE score, Finger Extension Y/N, Trunk Control Test, DM/HTN/AF, Time to assess | Bedside by trained PT, Blinded to outcome
  Baseline <72h   | NCCT: ASPECTS, CST involvement Y/N, Lesion volume cc, Side | Rated by radiologist blinded to outcome
  3 months ±2wk   | ARAT – Primary, FM-UE Hand, Box & Block, mRS | By PT blinded to baseline
 11.5 Study Procedure :
1. Screening in stroke unit, consent within 24h of admission
2. Baseline assessment <72h by PI/Co-I PT
3. NCCT downloaded from PACS, anonymized
4. Follow-up at 3 months in OPD/home visit
5. Data entry in REDCap


 11.7 Statistical Analysis :
Primary metric: AUC-ROC with 95% CI. Secondary: Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, Brier score, Calibration plot. Model comparison: DeLong test. Subgroup: Age>65, NIHSS>15, No FE. R + Python.

 12. EXPECTED OUTCOME OF PROJECT 
12.1  Primary : Validated ML model with AUC≥0.85, web calculator
12.2  Secondary : List of top 5 predictors for Indian stroke patients, open dataset
12.3  Translational : Tool for stroke units to decide rehab intensity by Day 3, reduce cost of unnecessary therapy

 13. FUTURE PLAN 
13.1  Phase 2 : RCT – Does using ML prediction improve 6mo outcomes vs usual care
13.2  Scale-up : Integrate with Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission, ABDM-compliant app
13.3  Extension : Add smartphone video of hand movement for deep learning – next grant

 14. EXPERTISE & INFRASTRUCTURE 
14.1  PI : 6 yrs stroke rehab experience, 5 PubMed papers on UE stroke, trained in ML via ICMR-NCDIR
14.2  Co-I Neuro : 500+ strokes/year, stroke unit head
14.3  Co-I ML : IIT graduate, 3 AI-healthcare projects
14.4  Institute : 1000-bedded hospital, 60 stroke admissions/month, dedicated stroke unit, IEC, DSIR, REDCap, GPU workstation available

 15. TIME FRAME 
  Year 1   |   Year 2  
Q1: IEC, SOP, Staff training | Q5: Complete follow-up n=350
Q2-Q4: Recruitment n=250, 3mo follow-up ongoing | Q6: Model development, internal validation
 | Q7: External validation n=150, Analysis
 | Q8: Manuscript, Web tool, Report

---

     PART III: DECLARATIONS 
 ETHICAL ISSUES :
IEC approval will be obtained before start. Written informed consent. No intervention. Data anonymized, DPDP Act 2023 compliant. No risk to patients.

content.text:
Here is the full extracted text from the document:

---

**PART I: PROJECT SUMMARY**

**1. Title of Project:**
Development and Validation of a Machine Learning-Based Prediction Model for Hand Function Recovery Using Routine Clinical and CT Data in Patients with Acute Ischemic Stroke

**Summary:**
Upper limb impairment affects 70% stroke survivors, with hand dysfunction being the primary cause of long-term disability. Early accurate prediction of hand recovery is essential for personalized rehabilitation but current tools like PREP2 require TMS/MRI unavailable in most Indian centers. A prospective cohort study is proposed to develop a machine learning model predicting 3-month hand function (ARAT ≥32) using only data routinely collected within 72h: NIHSS items, SAFE score, and non-contrast CT.

- 350 patients for model development, 150 for external validation
- Models: XGBoost, Random Forest, TabNet — compared against SAFE score and neurologist judgment
- SHAP for interpretability
- Primary outcome: AUC-ROC; hypothesis: ML model AUC ≥0.85 vs SAFE ~0.75
- Deliverable: free web-based calculator for bedside use
- Aligned with Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission

---

**PART II: TECHNICAL DETAILS**

**Origin of Proposal:**
Stroke is the 2nd leading cause of death and 3rd leading cause of disability in India. Existing algorithms (TMS/DTI) are not feasible in 90% of Indian hospitals. ML can leverage routine CT + clinical data for LMIC settings.

**Rationale:**
- 1.8 million new strokes/year in India
- No validated hand-specific ML model for Indian population using accessible data
- PREP2 not feasible; SAFE score alone has 25% misclassification
- Hypothesis: Multimodal ML integrating NIHSS, SAFE, and NCCT will outperform existing bedside tools

**Objectives:**
- Primary: Develop and internally validate an ML model predicting good hand function (ARAT ≥32) at 3 months using baseline clinical and NCCT data
- Secondary: Compare with SAFE score, logistic regression, and neurologist prediction; identify key predictors via SHAP; external validation; open-access web calculator

**Methodology:**
- Design: Prospective observational cohort, TRIPOD-AI compliant
- Inclusion: Age ≥18, first-ever acute ischemic stroke, admission <72h, NIHSS arm motor ≥1, pre-stroke mRS ≤2, consent
- Exclusion: Hemorrhagic stroke, bilateral infarcts, severe aphasia/neglect, terminal illness
- Sample size: n=350 development (140 events, 14 predictors at 10 EPV per Riley et al BMJ 2020), n=150 external validation; 20% loss to follow-up factored in

**Study Variables:**

| Time Point | Variables | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Baseline <72h | Age, Sex, Handedness, NIHSS total+items, SAFE score, Finger Extension Y/N, Trunk Control Test, DM/HTN/AF, Time to assess | Bedside by trained PT, blinded to outcome |
| Baseline <72h | NCCT: ASPECTS, CST involvement Y/N, Lesion volume (cc), Side | Rated by radiologist blinded to outcome |
| 3 months ±2wk | ARAT (primary), FM-UE Hand, Box & Block, mRS | By PT blinded to baseline |

**Procedure:**
1. Screening in stroke unit, consent within 24h of admission
2. Baseline assessment <72h by PI/Co-I PT
3. NCCT downloaded from PACS, anonymized
4. Follow-up at 3 months in OPD/home visit
5. Data entry in REDCap

**Statistical Analysis:**
- Primary metric: AUC-ROC with 95% CI
- Secondary: Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, Brier score, Calibration plot
- Model comparison: DeLong test
- Subgroups: Age >65, NIHSS >15, No finger extension
- Tools: R + Python

---

**Expected Outcomes:**
- Primary: Validated ML model with AUC ≥0.85 + web calculator
- Secondary: Top 5 predictors for Indian stroke patients, open dataset
- Translational: Tool for stroke units to decide rehab intensity by Day 3

**Future Plans:**
- Phase 2: RCT — does ML prediction improve 6-month outcomes vs usual care
- Scale-up: Integrate with ABDM-compliant app
- Extension: Add smartphone video of hand movement for deep learning (next grant)

**Expertise & Infrastructure:**
- PI: 6 years stroke rehab experience, 5 PubMed papers on UE stroke, ML trained via ICMR-NCDIR
- Co-I Neuro: 500+ strokes/year, stroke unit head
- Co-I ML: IIT graduate, 3 AI-healthcare projects
- Institute: 1000-bed hospital, 60 stroke admissions/month, dedicated stroke unit, IEC, DSIR, REDCap, GPU workstation

**Timeline:**

| Year 1 | Year 2 |
|---|---|
| Q1: IEC, SOP, Staff training | Q5: Complete follow-up n=350 |
| Q2–Q4: Recruitment n=250, ongoing follow-up | Q6: Model development, internal validation |
| | Q7: External validation n=150, Analysis |
| | Q8: Manuscript, Web tool, Report |

---

**PART III: DECLARATIONS**

**Ethical Issues:**
IEC approval before start. Written informed consent. No intervention. Data anonymized, DPDP Act 2023 compliant. No patient risk.

---

The extracted file has been saved to `C:\1stMLProject\docx_extracted.txt` for reference. The document is a grant/research proposal for an ICMR-style funding application for an Indian hospital-based ML study on stroke hand recovery prediction.
